title: Portal Hypertension
overview: Key clinically relevant changes from Baveno VI to Baveno VII in compensated advanced chronic liver disease and portal hypertension.

sources.name: Baveno VII – Renewing consensus in portal hypertension

updates.title: Non-invasive diagnosis of clinically significant portal hypertension
updates.summary: Baveno VII expands the role of transient elastography from mainly excluding high-risk varices to non-invasively ruling in or ruling out CSPH.
updates.oldRecommendation: Baveno VI mainly used liver stiffness and platelet count to identify compensated patients at very low risk of varices requiring treatment, allowing avoidance of screening endoscopy when LSM <20 kPa and platelets >150,000/mm3.
updates.newRecommendation: Baveno VII states that in virus- and/or alcohol-related cACLD and non-obese NASH-related cACLD, LSM by transient elastography <=15 kPa plus platelets >=150,000/mm3 rules out CSPH, while LSM >=25 kPa rules in CSPH.
updates.whatChanged: The non-invasive strategy moved beyond endoscopy triage and became a tool for CSPH risk stratification and treatment decisions.
updates.clinicalImpact: Clinicians can identify compensated patients who may benefit from earlier portal hypertension-directed therapy without requiring HVPG or immediate endoscopy in every case.

updates.title: NSBBs for prevention of first decompensation
updates.summary: Baveno VII emphasizes non-selective beta-blockers, especially carvedilol, to prevent first decompensation in patients with CSPH.
updates.oldRecommendation: Baveno VI focused NSBB use primarily on prevention of first variceal bleeding in patients with medium or large varices or high-risk varices.
updates.newRecommendation: Baveno VII recommends considering NSBBs for prevention of decompensation in patients with CSPH, with carvedilol preferred in compensated cirrhosis because of its greater portal pressure-lowering effect.
updates.whatChanged: The treatment target broadened from preventing variceal bleeding to preventing first hepatic decompensation in selected compensated patients with CSPH.
updates.clinicalImpact: A compensated patient with CSPH may be treated earlier, even before the traditional endpoint of high-risk varices becomes the sole trigger.

updates.title: Endoscopy avoidance criteria retained and reframed
updates.summary: The Baveno VI LSM <20 kPa plus platelet >150,000/mm3 rule remains clinically useful, but Baveno VII places it inside a broader CSPH-based pathway.
updates.oldRecommendation: Patients with cACLD, LSM <20 kPa and platelet count >150,000/mm3 could avoid screening endoscopy because the risk of varices requiring treatment was very low.
updates.newRecommendation: Baveno VII keeps non-invasive endoscopy-sparing strategies, but integrates them with CSPH diagnosis and NSBB decision-making rather than treating endoscopy avoidance as the only goal.
updates.whatChanged: The threshold itself was not the major change; the clinical context changed from variceal screening alone to portal hypertension risk stratification.
updates.clinicalImpact: Patients should not be managed only by asking whether they need endoscopy; clinicians should also ask whether they have CSPH and whether NSBB therapy is indicated.
updates.evidenceLevel: Baveno VI and VII consensus statements

updates.title: Spleen stiffness added as an adjunctive non-invasive tool
updates.summary: Baveno VII gives spleen stiffness a more explicit role in portal hypertension assessment when available.
updates.oldRecommendation: Baveno VI relied mainly on liver stiffness and platelet count for non-invasive triage.
updates.newRecommendation: Baveno VII includes spleen stiffness measurement as an additional non-invasive tool that can help refine the probability of CSPH and high-risk varices in selected patients.
updates.whatChanged: Non-invasive assessment expanded from a simple LSM-plus-platelet model toward a broader elastography-based risk assessment.
updates.clinicalImpact: Where available, spleen stiffness may reduce unnecessary endoscopy and improve risk classification, but it is not yet as universally available as liver stiffness.

updates.title: Follow-up after etiologic cure or suppression clarified
updates.summary: Baveno VII gives more practical guidance on reassessing portal hypertension risk after viral cure, viral suppression, alcohol abstinence, or other etiologic control.
updates.oldRecommendation: Baveno VI recognized that etiologic treatment could improve outcomes but provided less structured non-invasive follow-up logic after removal or control of the liver injury driver.
updates.newRecommendation: Baveno VII emphasizes repeat non-invasive reassessment after etiologic cure or suppression, because portal hypertension risk may decrease but does not automatically disappear in all patients.
updates.whatChanged: The newer guide gives a more dynamic model: portal hypertension surveillance can be adapted to regression of disease, but should not be stopped solely because the etiology is controlled.
updates.clinicalImpact: Patients with treated HCV, suppressed HBV, or alcohol abstinence still need individualized follow-up based on liver stiffness, platelets, and residual risk.
